ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic noodle dish class; no specific class for chicken feet noodle soup. Closest defensible parent class used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Soups and broths) is the closest defensible category for a noodle soup dish. No specific code exists for chicken feet noodle soup.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for chicken feet noodle soup. SNOMED CT does not enumerate individual prepared dish variants at this specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of chicken feet noodle soup weighing approximately 400 g, comprising braised chicken feet, egg noodles, bok choy, and soup broth.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Feet Noodles (Soup) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for the entry 'Chicken feet noodles (soup)' (cr_id F05060028). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — egg noodles as analogous food
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken feet noodle soup. GI estimated at 52 (low) based on egg noodles (GI ~52, University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021), which constitute the primary carbohydrate source. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (52) × available carbohydrate per serving (34 g for 400 g bowl) / 100 = 17.7. The soup broth and chicken feet contribute negligible additional carbohydrate. This is a conservative analogous estimate; actual GI may vary with noodle type and cooking time.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 318 mg/100 g, with a typical 400 g serving delivering approximately 1272 mg sodium — over half the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg — and minimal potassium or dietary fibre to offset vascular load.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to half a bowl, request reduced-salt broth and low-sodium soy sauce, and avoid drinking the soup liquid to substantially reduce sodium intake from this dish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate at 8.5 g/100 g from refined egg noodles with low dietary fibre (0.7 g/100 g) produces a moderate glycaemic load of approximately 17.7 per standard serving; sugar at 3.0 g/100 g from broth and sauces adds to postprandial glucose burden.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should request wholemeal or lower-GI noodle alternatives, increase the vegetable (bok choy) proportion to slow glucose absorption, limit the noodle portion to half, and avoid drinking all the broth to reduce sugar and sodium intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 318 mg/100 g (approximately 1272 mg per 400 g serving) poses a significant cardiovascular risk through blood pressure elevation; chicken feet are collagen-rich but also contain skin and connective tissue with moderate fat content (2.5 g/100 g), and saturated fat data are unavailable from the HPB source.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption, request low-sodium broth, avoid drinking the soup liquid, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to improve the overall dietary fat and fibre profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Feet Noodle Soup is a popular Singaporean Chinese hawker dish consisting of braised chicken feet served with egg noodles in a savoury soup broth, typically accompanied by bok choy and seasoned with soy sauce, garlic, and ginger. The chicken feet are slow-braised until the skin and tendons become gelatinous and tender, yielding a collagen-rich texture that is soft and slightly sticky. The broth is typically soy-based and mildly spiced. Per 100 g, the dish provides 72 kcal, 4.0 g protein, 2.5 g fat, 8.5 g carbohydrate, 3.0 g sugar, 0.7 g dietary fibre, and 318 mg sodium. The glycaemic index is estimated at 52 (low) based on egg noodles as the primary carbohydrate source, but the glycaemic load per standard 400 g bowl is approximately 17.7, placing it in the moderate range. Clinically, the dish is high in sodium and unsuitable for hypertension patients without modification; the moderate glycaemic load and low fibre content require portion control for type 2 diabetes management; and the sodium load is a cardiovascular concern. The dish is halal-certified in most hawker settings and is a common working lunch option in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05060028) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. This is an exact match to the canonical Singapore food composition record. GI is an analogous estimate based on egg noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source.
notes: Fields set to -1 indicate data not reported in the HPB source for this dish: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed fatty acid, micronutrient, and moisture data for this composite dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026. Calcium (9.0 mg/100 g) and iron (0.1 mg/100 g) are reported by HPB and included. The input record incorrectly classified the cuisine as 'western'; this has been corrected to 'Singaporean Chinese' based on the dish's established culinary origin. The input GI value of 0 with confidence 0.389 has been replaced with an analogous estimate of 52 (low) based on egg noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source per Project Veritas v2 policy. Halal status is retained from the input dietary tags. The dish is not suitable for strict vegetarian or vegan diets due to chicken content.
